revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八宗兼学]]
'''Hasshū Kengaku''' (八宗兼学) refers to the study of the doctrines of eight Buddhist sects in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Japan's Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}}. By extension, it also means learning and understanding things thoroughly. == Eight Sects == The eight sects referred to here are the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|qid=Q5366159|lt=Six Schools of Nara Buddhism|1=Nanto Rokushū}} and two esoteric sects that flourished in Japan from the Nara to the Heian period. ; Six Schools of Nara Buddhism * {{ill|Kusha-shū|en|Kusha-shū|qid=Q1782479|lt=Kusha-shū|1=Kusha-shū}} * {{ill|Jōjitsu-shū|ja|成実宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōjitsu-shū|1=Chengshi school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1350522}} * [[:en:Risshū (Buddhism)|Ritsu-shū]] * {{ill|East Asian Yogācāra|en|East Asian Yogācāra|qid=Q349849|lt=Hossō-shū|1=East Asian Yogācāra}} * {{ill|East Asian Mādhyamaka|en|East Asian Mādhyamaka|qid=Q1206580|lt=Sanron-shū|1=East Asian Mādhyamaka}} * {{ill|Huayan|en|Huayan|qid=Q1198075|lt=Kegon-shū|1=Huayan}} ; Esoteric Buddhism * {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai-shū|1=Tiantai}} * {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon-shū|1=Shingon Buddhism}} In some cases, instead of the Six Schools of Nara Buddhism, the six sects of {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|ja|鎌倉仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura Buddhism|qq=}} are included to make up the eight sects.
== Explanation == Hasshū Kengaku is widely practiced in {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai-shū|1=Tiantai}} in Japan. {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|qid=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}} traveled to {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|en|Tiantai Mountain|qid=Q1146118|lt=Mount Tiantai|1=Tiantai Mountain}} in the 24th year of the Enryaku era ({{ill|805|en|805|qid=Q30389|lt=805|1=805}}), where he studied the teachings of the Tiantai school before returning to Japan to found the Tendai sect. Saichō found it suspicious that esoteric Buddhism ({{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}}) was not included in the teachings of the Tiantai school and attempted to integrate it into the sect. Saichō sought to study esoteric Buddhism under {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}, but Kūkai refused, causing the initial attempt to incorporate esoteric Buddhism into the Tendai sect to falter. However, through the efforts of Saichō's prominent disciples, {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|qid=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}} and {{ill|Enchin|en|Enchin|qid=Q1972505|lt=Enchin|1=Enchin}}, esoteric Buddhism was successfully imported, leading to the establishment of '''Tendai Esoteric Buddhism'''. Tendai Esoteric Buddhism centered around the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|en|Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}} and incorporated elements of esoteric Buddhism, {{ill|Zen|ja|禅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen|1=禪|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23997305}}, {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nembutsu|1=Nianfo}}, and {{ill|Precepts|ja|戒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Precepts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Śīla|qid=Q1047844}}. By embracing these various teachings, the Tendai sect sought to become a "comprehensive Buddhism of Japan." {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} became a training center where the four teachings—Tendai doctrine, Zen, Nembutsu, and esoteric Buddhism—were studied together. This led to its reputation as a "'''Shishū Kengaku''' (Four-Sect Integrated Learning) training monastery," and academically, the study of the Six Schools of Nara Buddhism, Tendai, and Shingon became known as '''Hasshū Kengaku'''. In the Kamakura period, various new Buddhist sects such as {{ill|Nichiren-shū|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren-shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q206611}}, {{ill|Sōtō|en|Sōtō|qid=Q749085|lt=Sōtō-shū|1=Sōtō}}, {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai-shū|1=Rinzai school}}, {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|qid=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}}, and {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|en|Jōdo Shinshū|qid=Q912221|lt=Jōdo Shinshū|1=Jōdo Shinshū}} emerged from monks who had studied at Enryaku-ji. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tendai Jimon Sect|ja|天台寺門宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai Jimon Sect|1=Tendaijimon Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11442373}}, which follows the lineage of Enchin, emphasizes the integrated study of five principles: Enkyō (Lotus Sutra teachings), Precepts, Esoteric Buddhism, Zen, and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. Separately, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|qid=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}} has been a temple practicing the study of the Six Schools of Nara Buddhism since its founding. During the Heian period, the establishment of Tendai and Shingon temples within its precincts led it to become a temple of Hasshū Kengaku. == Major Temples Practicing Hasshū Kengaku == * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|qid=Q339859|lt=Shitennō-ji|1=Shitennō-ji}} * {{ill|Katsuō-ji|en|Katsuō-ji|qid=Q2747522|lt=Katsuo-ji|1=Katsuō-ji}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|qid=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}} * {{ill|Kegon-ji|ja|華厳寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kegon-ji|1=Kegon-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620047}} * {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|qid=Q189640|lt=Zenkō-ji|1=Zenkō-ji}} * {{ill|Ōyama-dera (Isehara)|ja|大山寺 (伊勢原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera (Isehara)|1=Ōyama-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434610}} * {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|qid=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gyōnen|en|Gyōnen|qid=Q3122771|lt=Gyonen|1=Gyōnen}} – Author of "{{ill|Hasshū Kōyō|ja|八宗綱要|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshū Kōyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" * {{ill|Thirteen Sects and Fifty-Six Schools|ja|十三宗五十六派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thirteen Sects and Fifty-Six Schools|1=Thirteen Sects and Fifty-Six Schools|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6139407}} {{Buddhism-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|八宗兼学|version=103172773|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!-- The commonly known ones are: * {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai-shū|1=Tiantai}} (Founder in Japan: {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|qid=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}) * {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon-shū|1=Shingon Buddhism}} (Founder: {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}) * {{ill|Nichiren-shū|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren-shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Founder: {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478ja|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q31147812=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) * {{ill|Sōtō|en|Sōtō|qid=Q749085|lt=Sōtō-shū|1=Sōtō}} (Founder in Japan: {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|qid=Q318064|lt=Dōgen|1=Dōgen}}) * {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai-shū|1=Rinzai school}} (Founder in Japan: {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|qid=Q366128|lt=Eisai|1=Eisai}}) * {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|qid=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}} (Founder: {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}}) * {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|en|Jōdo Shinshū|qid=Q912221|lt=Jōdo Shinshū|1=Jōdo Shinshū}} (Founder: {{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}}) * {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|qid=Q696966|lt=Ji-shū|1=Ji-shu}} (Founder: {{ill|Ippen|en|Ippen|qid=Q1056232|lt=Ippen|1=Ippen}}) --> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11390764}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist enumerations of 8]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Tiantai]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
